title: Trinidadian pepper sauce
subtitle: Scotch bonnet hot sauce with mustard and turmeric
excerpt: Scotch bonnets blended with vinegar, mustard, garlic, turmeric, and a little sugar to a thick, fiery, golden-yellow sauce. Trinidadian pepper sauce is milder and more complex than a straight hot sauce — the mustard and turmeric give it body and a warm earthiness, the vinegar cuts the heat, and the sugar rounds the edges. It is the condiment on the Trinidadian table at every meal.
sourceNotes: Trinidadian pepper sauce is distinct from Jamaican and Barbadian hot sauces in its use of mustard and turmeric, which gives the sauce its characteristic golden colour and earthy note. The sauce appears in Trinidadian culinary literature and is associated with brands like Chief's and Matouk's. Home-made versions vary from very mild to extreme depending on whether the seeds are included.

recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple for a larger batch. Keeps for 2 months in the fridge.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves after 24 hours as the flavours meld. Make the day before if possible.

content.text: Wear gloves when handling the scotch bonnets. The capsaicin in scotch bonnets is significantly more concentrated than in jalapeños or bird's eye chillies, and it will burn if transferred to eyes or sensitive skin. Including the seeds produces a very hot sauce; deseeding before blending produces a milder result that still has significant heat.

The sauce keeps for up to 2 months in a sterilised jar in the fridge. It will separate — stir or shake before use. The heat level softens slightly over the first week as the vinegar works on the peppers.

If including the {{carrot}}, boil it in water for 5 minutes until just tender and drain. Place the {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{garlic}}, cooked carrot (if using), {{white-wine-vinegar}}, {{turmeric}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a blender. Blend to a smooth sauce, adding 2 to 3 tablespoons of water if needed to help the blades move. Taste for balance — adjust vinegar for sharpness, sugar to round the heat, salt. Pour into a sterilised jar. Store in the fridge.

items.cause: Seeds included on very hot scotch bonnets
items.fix: Deseed the scotch bonnets before blending for a milder sauce. Adding more carrot also dilutes the heat without adding acidity
Add water only a tablespoon at a time. The carrot (if included) thickens the sauce naturally; without carrot the sauce is thinner

items.cause: Scotch bonnet seeds included in high quantity
items.fix: Seeds add more bitterness than flesh. Remove them next time or use fewer scotch bonnets and more carrot for a balanced result

content.text: Pepper sauce is the condiment on every Trinidadian table. It is added to doubles, to stew chicken, to macaroni pie, to roti — to almost everything. The Trinidadian style (mustard, turmeric, vinegar) is distinct from the Barbadian style (scotch bonnet in vinegar, very simple) and from the Jamaican scotch bonnet sauces. Commercial brands like Matouk's and Chief's define the style; home-made versions are often more irregular and either much milder or much hotter depending on family preference.
